name: poloidal_magnetic_flux_of_plasma_boundary
description: Poloidal magnetic flux evaluated at the last closed flux surface (plasma boundary), defining the edge of the confined plasma.
documentation: The poloidal magnetic flux $\psi_{\mathrm{bnd}}$ (in Wb) at the plasma boundary — the last closed flux surface (LCFS) or separatrix. Note: although the DD path label mentions 'toroidal flux', this standard name refers to the poloidal magnetic flux $\psi$ at the boundary. In equilibrium reconstruction, $\psi_{\mathrm{bnd}}$ is a critical output that defines the plasma edge. For a limiter-bounded plasma, the boundary coincides with the limiter contact point; for a diverted plasma, it corresponds to the separatrix passing through the X-point. The value of $\psi_{\mathrm{bnd}}$ is COCOS-dependent and its sign follows the convention for poloidal flux in the chosen coordinate system. In normalized flux coordinates, $\psi_N = (\psi - \psi_{\mathrm{axis}}) / (\psi_{\mathrm{bnd}} - \psi_{\mathrm{axis}})$, so this quantity anchors the $\psi_N = 1$ surface. Typical absolute values depend strongly on machine size and plasma current; for large tokamaks, $|\psi_{\mathrm{bnd}}|$ can reach tens of Wb. Determined by equilibrium reconstruction codes using magnetic diagnostics and internal constraints.

name: convergence_iteration_count
description: Number of iterations completed in the convergence loop of an equilibrium solver.
documentation: This dimensionless integer records how many iterations were performed before the equilibrium solver terminated its convergence loop, either by satisfying the convergence criterion or by reaching a maximum iteration limit. In iterative Grad–Shafranov solvers (e.g., EFIT, LIUQE, NICE), the Picard or Newton iteration count is a key diagnostic of solution quality: typical well-converged free-boundary reconstructions require 10–50 iterations, while poor initial guesses or pathological profiles may need hundreds. Monitoring this quantity helps identify time slices where the equilibrium solution may not be fully converged. Values significantly above the solver's typical range suggest numerical difficulties and warrant inspection of the residual error.

name: poloidal_component_of_magnetic_field
description: Poloidal component of the magnetic field, representing the field in the $(R, Z)$ plane perpendicular to the toroidal direction.
documentation: The poloidal magnetic field $B_\theta$ is the component of $\mathbf{B}$ lying in the $(R, Z)$ plane, perpendicular to the toroidal ($\hat{\phi}$) direction. It is related to the cylindrical components by $B_\theta = \sqrt{B_R^2 + B_Z^2}$ (magnitude) or, more precisely, its signed projection along the local poloidal unit vector. In a tokamak, $B_\theta$ is generated primarily by the plasma current $I_p$ and is governed by Ampère's law: $\oint B_\theta \, dl = \mu_0 I_{\mathrm{enc}}$. Typical values are $B_\theta \sim 0.1$–$1.0$ T at the plasma edge for devices with $I_p \sim 0.5$–$15$ MA. In the context of equilibrium reconstruction, this quantity represents the reconstructed poloidal field at magnetic probe locations, computed from the solution of the Grad–Shafranov equation and compared against measured probe values. Positive $B_\theta$ follows the poloidal angle direction consistent with the chosen COCOS convention.

name: major_radius_of_reference_position
description: Major radius $R$ of a reference measurement or constraint position in the tokamak midplane coordinate system.
documentation: This quantity specifies the major radius $R$ (in meters) of a reference point in the $(R, Z)$ cylindrical coordinate system of the tokamak. In the equilibrium reconstruction context, it typically identifies the spatial location at which a constraint measurement (e.g., parallel current density $j_\parallel$, pressure, or magnetic field) is evaluated or prescribed. Accurate knowledge of measurement positions is critical for constraint-based Grad–Shafranov solvers, as small errors in $R$ propagate into reconstructed profiles. For medium to large tokamaks, $R$ values range from the high-field side ($R \sim R_0 - a$) to the low-field side ($R \sim R_0 + a$), where $R_0$ is the geometric major radius and $a$ is the minor radius (e.g., $R \in [4.5, 8.5]$ m for ITER). The value must be strictly positive since it represents a radial distance from the machine symmetry axis.

name: contravariant_metric_tensor_g11
description: The (1,1) component $g^{11}$ of the contravariant metric tensor for the equilibrium coordinate system.
documentation:
The contravariant metric tensor component $g^{11}$ (dimensionless) is the (1,1) element of the inverse metric tensor $g^{ij}$ associated with the curvilinear coordinate system used to describe the magnetic equilibrium. For a coordinate system $(u^1, u^2, u^3)$, the contravariant metric tensor is defined by $g^{ij} = 
abla u^i \cdot 
abla u^j$; thus $g^{11} = |
abla u^1|^2$, where $u^1$ is the first coordinate (typically a radial-like flux coordinate). This quantity is essential for expressing differential operators—such as the Laplacian, divergence, and curl—in flux coordinates, which are required for solving MHD equilibrium and stability equations. It is computed internally by equilibrium reconstruction or MHD codes on the coordinate grid defined by the equilibrium solver. The value of $g^{11}$ depends on the specific coordinate choice (e.g., Boozer, Hamada, or PEST coordinates) and varies across the poloidal cross-section. Related metric components $g^{22}$, $g^{33}$, and the off-diagonal elements complete the full tensor description of the coordinate geometry.

name: contravariant_metric_tensor_g12
description: The (1,2) off-diagonal component $g^{12}$ of the contravariant metric tensor for the equilibrium coordinate system.
documentation: The contravariant metric tensor component $g^{12}$ (dimensionless) is the off-diagonal element coupling the first and second coordinates in the inverse metric tensor $g^{ij}$ of the equilibrium coordinate system. It is defined as $g^{12} = \nabla u^1 \cdot \nabla u^2$, where $u^1$ and $u^2$ are the first two generalized coordinates (typically a radial flux-like coordinate and a poloidal angle-like coordinate). A nonzero $g^{12}$ indicates that the coordinate lines of $u^1$ and $u^2$ are not orthogonal at the given point. This component enters gradient and divergence operators in flux coordinates and is important for accurate representation of magnetic field-line geometry, parallel transport, and MHD stability calculations in non-orthogonal coordinate systems. It is computed by equilibrium reconstruction or MHD codes on the coordinate grid. In orthogonal coordinate systems (e.g., Boozer coordinates in the straight-field-line limit), $g^{12}$ may vanish or be small; in general geometry it is nonzero.

name: contravariant_metric_tensor_g13
description: The (1,3) off-diagonal component $g^{13}$ of the contravariant metric tensor for the equilibrium coordinate system.
documentation: The contravariant metric tensor component $g^{13}$ (dimensionless) is the off-diagonal element coupling the first and third coordinates in the inverse metric tensor $g^{ij}$. It is defined as $g^{13} = \nabla u^1 \cdot \nabla u^3$, where $u^1$ is typically a radial-like flux coordinate and $u^3$ is a toroidal angle-like coordinate. In an axisymmetric tokamak equilibrium with a standard $(\rho, \theta, \phi)$ coordinate system, the cross-coupling between radial and toroidal gradients is often zero due to axisymmetry; however, in 3-D stellarator equilibria or when non-standard coordinate mappings are used, $g^{13}$ can be nonzero and is essential for correctly computing field-line trajectories and transport coefficients. This component is output by equilibrium solvers on the computational grid. Together with the other eight independent metric components (for a 3-D system) it fully characterizes the local coordinate geometry.

name: contravariant_metric_tensor_g22
description: The (2,2) component of the contravariant metric tensor $g^{22}$ on the equilibrium coordinate grid, characterizing local geometry in the second coordinate direction.
documentation:
The contravariant metric tensor component $g^{22}$ relates gradients of the second generalized coordinate to physical distances in the equilibrium coordinate system defined by the grid type. For a coordinate system $(x^1, x^2, x^3)$, the contravariant metric tensor is defined as $g^{ij} = 
abla x^i \cdot 
abla x^j$, so $g^{22} = |
abla x^2|^2$. This component is essential for expressing differential operators (gradient, divergence, Laplacian) in curvilinear coordinates used in equilibrium reconstruction and MHD stability codes. It is dimensionless when coordinates are chosen appropriately, though its numerical value depends on the specific coordinate system (e.g., flux coordinates, Boozer coordinates). Typical values depend strongly on the grid type and machine geometry. Related tensor components provide the full geometric description of the coordinate system.

name: contravariant_metric_tensor_g33
description: The (3,3) component of the contravariant metric tensor $g^{33}$ on the equilibrium coordinate grid, characterizing the squared magnitude of the gradient of the third coordinate.
documentation:
The contravariant metric tensor component $g^{33}$ is defined as $g^{33} = 
abla x^3 \cdot 
abla x^3$, where $x^3$ is the third coordinate of the equilibrium grid (typically the toroidal angle $\phi$). In flux-aligned coordinates $(ho, 	heta, \phi)$, $g^{33} = |
abla \phi|^2 = 1/R^2$ for standard cylindrical-toroidal coordinates, where $R$ is the major-radial distance from the axis of symmetry. This component is essential for computing the toroidal contribution to differential operators such as the Laplacian, the magnetic field magnitude, and the current density in curvilinear form. The contravariant metric is the matrix inverse of the covariant metric: $g^{ij} g_{jk} = \delta^i_k$. For axisymmetric tokamak equilibria in standard coordinates, $g^{33} \sim 1/R^2$ with typical values of order $0.1$–$0.5\;\mathrm{m}^{-2}$ depending on major radius (note: the dimensionless unit reported here reflects a specific coordinate normalization). Equilibrium codes such as CHEASE, VMEC, and HELENA output this quantity as part of the grid geometry. The Jacobian satisfies $J = 1/\sqrt{\det(g^{ij})}$, connecting all contravariant components.

name: covariant_metric_tensor_g11
description: The (1,1) component $g_{11}$ of the covariant metric tensor for the equilibrium coordinate system.
documentation: The covariant metric tensor component $g_{11}$ (dimensionless) is the (1,1) element of the metric tensor $g_{ij}$ for the curvilinear coordinate system used in the magnetic equilibrium description. It is defined as $g_{ij} = \mathbf{e}_i \cdot \mathbf{e}_j$, where $\mathbf{e}_i = \partial \mathbf{r}/\partial u^i$ are the covariant basis vectors, so $g_{11} = |\mathbf{e}_1|^2$. This quantity encodes the local stretching of the first coordinate direction and is needed for computing arc lengths, surface areas, and volumes in flux coordinates, as well as for formulating the MHD equations in generalized geometry. It is produced by equilibrium solvers alongside the contravariant components and the Jacobian $\sqrt{g}$. The covariant and contravariant metric tensors are inverses of each other: $g_{ik}\,g^{kj} = \delta_i^{\,j}$. The numerical values depend on the choice of coordinate system and normalization conventions used by the equilibrium code.

name: covariant_metric_tensor_g12
description: The (1,2) off-diagonal component $g_{12}$ of the covariant metric tensor for the equilibrium coordinate system.
documentation: The covariant metric tensor component $g_{12}$ (dimensionless) is the off-diagonal element of the metric tensor $g_{ij}$ coupling the first and second coordinate directions. It is defined as $g_{12} = \mathbf{e}_1 \cdot \mathbf{e}_2$, where $\mathbf{e}_i = \partial \mathbf{r}/\partial u^i$ are the covariant basis vectors. A nonzero value indicates non-orthogonality of the $u^1$ and $u^2$ coordinate lines. This component is required for evaluating scalar products, differential operators, and volume elements in generalized flux coordinates used by MHD equilibrium and transport codes. The full covariant metric tensor $g_{ij}$ is the matrix inverse of the contravariant tensor $g^{ij}$. Values depend on the specific coordinate system choice and the plasma shape; in highly shaped plasmas with strong triangularity, off-diagonal metric components can be significant.

name: covariant_metric_tensor_g13
description: The (1,3) component of the covariant metric tensor $g_{13}$ on the equilibrium coordinate grid, encoding cross-coupling between the first and third coordinate directions.
documentation:
The covariant metric tensor component $g_{13}$ quantifies the inner product between the coordinate basis vectors $\mathbf{e}_1$ and $\mathbf{e}_3$ of the curvilinear grid used in equilibrium reconstruction: $g_{13} = \mathbf{e}_1 \cdot \mathbf{e}_3$. In tokamak and stellarator equilibrium codes, the coordinate system is typically $(ho, 	heta, \phi)$ or similar flux-aligned coordinates, where $ho$ is a radial-like label, $	heta$ a poloidal-like angle, and $\phi$ the toroidal angle. Off-diagonal covariant components such as $g_{13}$ are essential for correctly computing differential operators ($
abla$, $
abla 	imes$, $
abla \cdot$) in non-orthogonal grids and appear in the MHD equilibrium and stability equations. This component is dimensionless (unit 1) because it represents a metric coefficient in general curvilinear coordinates normalized appropriately by the coordinate system definition. In orthogonal coordinate systems $g_{13} = 0$; non-zero values arise when the grid includes shear between the radial-like and toroidal-like directions. The Jacobian of the coordinate system satisfies $J = \sqrt{\det(g_{ij})}$, linking all metric components. Typical values depend on the specific coordinate normalization and machine geometry. This quantity is computed during equilibrium reconstruction by codes such as EFIT, CHEASE, or VMEC.

description: The (2,3) component of the covariant metric tensor $g_{23}$ on the equilibrium coordinate grid, coupling the second and third coordinate directions.
documentation:

In flux-aligned coordinate systems $(ho, 	heta, \phi)$, this off-diagonal element encodes coupling between the poloidal-like ($	heta$) and toroidal ($\phi$) directions. Non-zero $g_{23}$ arises naturally in non-orthogonal grids, such as those using straight-field-line angles or Boozer coordinates, where the poloidal angle is chosen so that field lines appear straight on the $(	heta, \phi)$ plane. This component enters all curvilinear differential operators and is essential for computing quantities such as the parallel gradient $
abla_\parallel$, the magnetic field representation $\mathbf{B} = 
abla \alpha 	imes 
abla \psi$ in covariant form, and the Jacobian $J = \sqrt{\det(g_{ij})}$. The quantity is dimensionless (unit 1). It is produced by equilibrium reconstruction codes (EFIT, CHEASE, VMEC, HELENA) as part of the coordinate system description. Typical magnitudes depend on the coordinate normalization and on the degree of non-orthogonality of the chosen grid.

name: covariant_metric_tensor_g33
description: The (3,3) component of the covariant metric tensor $g_{33}$ on the equilibrium coordinate grid, representing the squared line element along the third coordinate direction.
documentation: The covariant metric tensor component $g_{33}$ is defined as $g_{33} = \mathbf{e}_3 \cdot \mathbf{e}_3$, where $\mathbf{e}_3 = \partial \mathbf{r}/\partial x^3$ is the natural basis vector of the third coordinate. In standard flux-aligned tokamak coordinates $(\rho, \theta, \phi)$, $g_{33} = R^2$ where $R$ is the major-radial distance, because the toroidal arc-length element is $\mathrm{d}l_\phi = R\,\mathrm{d}\phi$. This diagonal component enters the computation of the magnetic field covariant representation $B_\phi = \mathbf{B}\cdot\mathbf{e}_3$, the Jacobian $J = \sqrt{\det(g_{ij})}$, and all volume and surface integrals in curvilinear geometry. The quantity is dimensionless (unit 1), reflecting the chosen coordinate normalization. For a large tokamak with $R \approx 6\;\mathrm{m}$ using unnormalized coordinates, $g_{33} \approx 36\;\mathrm{m}^2$; with normalized coordinates the numerical value depends on the normalization convention. The covariant and contravariant metrics are matrix inverses: $g_{ij} g^{jk} = \delta_i^k$. Equilibrium codes (EFIT, CHEASE, VMEC, HELENA) compute and export all metric components as part of the coordinate system specification.

name: faraday_angle_constraint_weight
description: Dimensionless weight assigned to a Faraday rotation angle measurement in the equilibrium reconstruction χ² minimization.
documentation: Standard χ² weight controlling the relative importance of a Faraday rotation angle measurement in the equilibrium reconstruction. In the cost function $\chi^2 = \sum_i w_i (y_i^{\mathrm{meas}} - y_i^{\mathrm{recon}})^2$, the weight $w_i$ (dimensionless) for the Faraday angle channel scales the contribution of the mismatch between the measured and reconstructed Faraday rotation angle $\alpha_F$. Larger weights force the equilibrium solver to match the corresponding polarimetry measurement more closely, effectively reducing the allowable residual on that channel. Typical values depend on the inverse of the measurement variance: $w_i \propto 1/\sigma_i^2$, and are set by the diagnostic uncertainty, usually of order $10^2$–$10^6$ depending on normalization conventions. The associated physical measurement is `faraday_rotation_angle`.

name: faraday_rotation_angle
description: Rotation of the polarization plane of an electromagnetic probe beam traversing a magnetized plasma, caused by the Faraday effect.
documentation: The Faraday rotation angle $\alpha_F$ quantifies the cumulative rotation of the polarization plane of a linearly polarized probe beam (typically far-infrared or CO$_2$ laser) as it propagates through a magnetized plasma. It is governed by the line integral $\alpha_F = \frac{e^3 \lambda^2}{8\pi^2 \epsilon_0 m_e^2 c^3} \int n_e(\ell)\, B_{\parallel}(\ell)\, d\ell$, where $n_e$ is the electron number density (m$^{-3}$), $B_{\parallel}$ is the magnetic field component parallel to the beam (T), $\lambda$ is the probe wavelength (m), $e$ is the electron charge, $m_e$ the electron mass, $c$ the speed of light, and $\ell$ the path length (m). Faraday rotation polarimetry is a key internal diagnostic for constraining the poloidal magnetic field and current density profiles in equilibrium reconstruction codes such as EFIT and LIUQE. Typical Faraday angles in tokamak plasmas range from a few degrees to tens of degrees depending on density, field strength, and chord geometry. Both measured and reconstructed values of this angle are used in equilibrium fitting; the constraint weight is given by `faraday_angle_constraint_weight`.

name: flux_loop_constraint_measurement_time
description: Time instant at which the flux loop measurement was sampled or interpolated for use as an equilibrium reconstruction constraint.
documentation: Represents the exact time slice extracted from the flux loop diagnostic time array and used as a constraint in the equilibrium reconstruction. If the requested time does not coincide with an existing sample in the source data, linear interpolation between adjacent samples is applied. This timestamp enables traceability between the reconstructed equilibrium and the raw diagnostic data, ensuring that the temporal alignment of constraints is well documented. Typical values span the duration of a plasma discharge, from 0 to several seconds in most tokamak experiments. Standard χ² weight controlling the relative importance of this measurement in the equilibrium reconstruction is handled by a separate weight quantity.

name: flux_surface_averaged_inverse_major_radius
description: Flux-surface average of $1/R$, a fundamental geometric metric coefficient in toroidal equilibrium and transport formulations.
documentation: Defined as $\langle 1/R \rangle$, where $R$ (in m) is the major radius and angle brackets denote a flux-surface average over the poloidal cross-section of a given flux surface. This metric coefficient (often labeled $gm9$) appears in the toroidal formulation of Ohm's law, the safety factor definition $q = (1/2\pi) \oint (B_\phi / R) \, dl_p / B_p$, and various neoclassical transport expressions. For a large tokamak with $R_0 \approx 6$ m, $\langle 1/R \rangle \sim 0.17$ m$^{-1}$ near the magnetic axis, increasing toward the high-field side-weighted value at outer flux surfaces due to the Shafranov shift. Computed routinely by Grad–Shafranov equilibrium solvers (EFIT, CHEASE, HELENA) as part of the 1-D profile output on each flux surface. Related metric coefficients include $\langle 1/R^2 \rangle$ and $\langle R^2 \rangle$.

name: flux_surface_averaged_inverse_major_radius_squared
description: Flux-surface average of $1/R^2$, a geometric metric coefficient used in toroidal plasma equilibrium and transport calculations.
documentation: Defined as $\langle 1/R^2 \rangle$, where $R$ (in m) is the major radius and the angle brackets denote a flux-surface average: $\langle A \rangle = \oint A \, dS / \oint dS$ over the poloidal cross-section of a flux surface. This quantity is one of the standard geometric metric coefficients (often labeled $gm1$) that appear in the flux-surface-averaged forms of the MHD equilibrium and transport equations. It enters, for example, the neoclassical parallel current expression and the Ohmic heating formula $P_{\Omega} = \eta \langle j^2 \rangle$, where the current density must be weighted by $\langle 1/R^2 \rangle$. Typical values range from roughly $0.05$ to $0.5$ m$^{-2}$ depending on machine size and Shafranov shift; for a large tokamak with $R_0 \approx 6$ m, $\langle 1/R^2 \rangle \sim 0.03$ m$^{-2}$. Computed by equilibrium solvers (e.g., EFIT, CHEASE, HELENA) on each flux surface.

name: flux_surface_averaged_inverse_major_radius_squared_toroidal_flux_gradient_squared
description: Flux-surface average of $|\nabla \rho_{\mathrm{tor}}|^2 / R^2$, a composite metric coefficient in tokamak equilibrium and transport equations.
documentation: Defined as $\langle |\nabla \rho_{\mathrm{tor}}|^2 / R^2 \rangle$, where $\rho_{\mathrm{tor}}$ is the normalized toroidal flux coordinate, $R$ (in m) is the major radius, and angle brackets denote a flux-surface average. This composite geometric metric coefficient (often labeled $gm2$ in equilibrium codes) appears prominently in the flux-coordinate formulation of tokamak transport equations. For instance, the diffusive particle flux in the transport equation for species density $n$ on a flux surface takes the form $\Gamma = -D \, \langle |\nabla \rho_{\mathrm{tor}}|^2 / R^2 \rangle \, \partial n / \partial \rho_{\mathrm{tor}}$, making this coefficient essential for coupling 1-D transport solvers to 2-D equilibria. Typical values depend on machine geometry and are computed by equilibrium reconstruction or fixed-boundary equilibrium solvers such as CHEASE, HELENA, or EFIT. On the magnetic axis the gradient vanishes, so the quantity tends to zero; at the edge it reaches its largest values.

name: flux_surface_averaged_major_radius
description: Flux-surface average of the major radius coordinate $R$, characterizing the radial center-of-mass of each flux surface.
documentation:
The flux-surface averaged major radius $\langle R \rangle$ (in meters) is defined as the integral of the major radius $R$ over a closed flux surface divided by the surface area:
$$\langle R \rangle = \frac{\oint R \, dA}{\oint dA},$$
where $dA$ is the surface area element. This geometric quantity captures the Shafranov shift and shaping effects on each flux surface and is one of the standard geometric metric coefficients (often denoted $gm8$) used in flux-coordinate-based transport and equilibrium calculations.

In tokamaks $\langle R \rangle$ varies from the magnetic axis value $R_0$ at the core to a value near the geometric center of the separatrix at the edge. For typical large tokamaks, $\langle R \rangle$ ranges from approximately 1.5 m (compact devices) to 6.2 m (ITER). The outward Shafranov shift causes $\langle R \rangle$ on inner flux surfaces to exceed the geometric major radius of the corresponding surface at the midplane.

This quantity appears naturally in the metric coefficients required for solving the Grad-Shafranov equation in flux coordinates and in neoclassical transport theory. It is computed by equilibrium reconstruction codes from the full 2-D poloidal flux map $\psi(R,Z)$.

abla ho_{\mathrm{tor}}|^2 angle \approx 1$, and it deviates from unity at larger radii due to elongation, triangularity, and Shafranov shift. Equilibrium solvers compute this metric coefficient on each flux surface alongside related quantities such as $\langle |
abla ho_{\mathrm{tor}}| angle$ and $\langle 1/B^2 angle$.

name: flux_surface_cross_sectional_area_gradient_over_toroidal_flux_coordinate
description: Radial derivative of the flux-surface cross-sectional area with respect to the toroidal flux coordinate $\rho_\mathrm{tor}$.
documentation:
This quantity is defined as $dA/d\rho_\mathrm{tor}$, where $A$ is the poloidal cross-sectional area (m$^2$) enclosed by a flux surface and $\rho_\mathrm{tor} = \sqrt{\Phi / (\pi B_0)}$ is the toroidal flux coordinate (m), with $\Phi$ the toroidal magnetic flux (Wb) and $B_0$ the vacuum toroidal field at the magnetic axis (T). The derivative has units of meters.

This geometric profile quantity is essential for volume integration in flux coordinates, since it enters the Jacobian connecting $\rho_\mathrm{tor}$ to real-space volume. It is computed by equilibrium reconstruction codes (e.g., EFIT, CHEASE) as part of the metric output and is used in transport modeling to convert between flux-surface-averaged and volume-integrated quantities. Values are zero at the magnetic axis and increase outward, reaching of order the poloidal circumference times the Shafranov-shift-corrected minor radius at the last closed flux surface—typically $1$–$10$ m for medium-to-large tokamaks.

name: iron_core_segment_radial_magnetization_constraint_measurement_time
description: Time at which the radial magnetization of an iron core segment was sampled for use as an equilibrium reconstruction constraint.
documentation: This quantity records the exact time instant at which the radial ($R$-direction) magnetization measurement of an iron core segment is extracted from its source diagnostic time array. When the requested time does not coincide with a discrete sample in the source data, linear interpolation between neighboring samples is applied. Iron core segments are present in many tokamak designs (e.g., transformer cores) and their magnetization state influences the poloidal flux distribution, making it relevant as a constraint in Grad–Shafranov equilibrium solvers. The measurement time is expressed in seconds and must fall within the temporal range of the source diagnostic. Related constraint quantities include the measured value, the reconstructed value, and the associated $\chi^2$ weight.

name: magnetic_shear
description: Dimensionless measure of the radial variation of the safety factor, defined as $s = (\rho_{\text{tor}}/q)\,dq/d\rho_{\text{tor}}$.
documentation: The magnetic shear $s$ quantifies how rapidly the safety factor $q$ changes across flux surfaces and is defined as $s = (\rho_{\text{tor}}/q)\,dq/d\rho_{\text{tor}}$, where $\rho_{\text{tor}}$ (in m) is the square root of the normalized toroidal magnetic flux and $q$ is the safety factor (dimensionless). It plays a central role in MHD stability theory: negative or low shear in the core can trigger internal transport barriers, while strong positive shear near the edge stabilizes ballooning modes via the second stability access criterion. The magnetic shear profile is obtained from equilibrium reconstruction or from direct measurement of the $q$-profile using motional Stark effect (MSE) diagnostics. Typical values range from $s \approx 0$ near the magnetic axis (where $q$ has a local extremum) to $s \approx 1$–$3$ near the plasma edge. Reversed shear ($s < 0$) is characteristic of advanced tokamak scenarios with internal transport barriers. The interplay between shear and pressure gradient drives interchange and ballooning instabilities; the Mercier criterion involves $s$ explicitly. Related profiles include the safety factor and the normalized toroidal magnetic flux coordinate.

name: major_radius_of_current_center
description: Major radius of the plasma current centroid, computed as the toroidal-current-density-weighted average of $R$ over the poloidal cross section.
documentation: The major radius of the current center is defined as $R_{\text{cc}} = \frac{1}{I_p} \int j_\phi \, R \, dS$, where $j_\phi$ is the toroidal current density in A/m², $R$ is the major radius in m, $I_p$ is the total plasma current in A, and the integral is taken over the poloidal cross section $dS$. This quantity characterizes the radial position of the effective current channel and is a key global equilibrium parameter. A shift of the current center inward or outward relative to the geometric axis reflects changes in the internal current profile (e.g., peaking or broadening). It is routinely obtained from equilibrium reconstruction codes such as EFIT or LIUQE. In a typical large tokamak, $R_{\text{cc}}$ is close to but not identical to the magnetic axis major radius; differences of a few centimeters are common depending on the pressure and current profiles. Typical values range from ~1.0 m (compact devices) to ~6.2 m (ITER).

name: major_radius_of_plasma_boundary_gap_reference_point
description: Major radius coordinate of the reference point used to define a plasma boundary gap measurement.
documentation:
The major radius $R$ (in meters) of the fixed reference point from which a plasma boundary gap is evaluated. A gap is defined as the distance between this reference point and the last closed flux surface (separatrix) along a specified direction in the poloidal cross-section. Gap reference points are typically located on the first wall or limiter structures and are chosen to monitor critical plasma-wall clearances during operation.

Gap measurements are essential inputs for plasma shape control systems, which adjust poloidal field coil currents in real time to maintain adequate clearance between the plasma boundary and plasma-facing components. In a typical tokamak, $R$ for gap reference points ranges from the inboard wall (~1.0 m in medium-sized devices) to outboard limiters (~2.5 m), depending on the machine geometry.

The gap direction and reference point position together uniquely define the gap measurement. Related quantities include the vertical coordinate of the reference point and the gap distance value itself.

name: mse_polarization_angle
description: Polarization angle of the motional Stark effect emission, encoding the local magnetic field pitch angle in the plasma.
documentation:
The MSE polarization angle $\gamma_\mathrm{MSE}$ is the angle of the linearly polarized $\sigma$-component of the Doppler-shifted Balmer-alpha emission produced when fast neutral beam atoms interact with the local magnetic field. It is related to the magnetic field pitch angle by $\gamma_\mathrm{MSE} \approx \arctan(B_Z / B_\phi)$ (corrected for beam geometry and Faraday rotation in optical elements), where $B_Z$ is the vertical field component (T) and $B_\phi$ is the toroidal field (T).

MSE is one of the most direct internal diagnostics of the safety factor profile $q(\rho)$ and current density distribution. Measured values typically range from $-0.3$ to $+0.3$ rad in standard tokamak equilibria, with higher pitch angles near the plasma edge. Both measured and equilibrium-reconstructed values are used in integrated modeling workflows. The quantity is expressed in radians.

name: mse_polarization_angle_constraint_weight
description: Dimensionless weight assigned to the MSE polarization angle measurement in the equilibrium reconstruction χ² cost function.
documentation:
Standard χ² weight controlling the relative importance of the motional Stark effect (MSE) polarization angle measurement in the equilibrium reconstruction. The MSE diagnostic measures the polarization direction of Doppler-shifted $H_\alpha$ (or $D_\alpha$) emission from a neutral beam, which is sensitive to the local magnetic field pitch angle $\gamma = \arctan(B_Z / B_\phi)$. A larger weight increases the influence of the corresponding MSE channel on the reconstructed current-density profile.

Typical values range from 0 (measurement ignored) to $O(1)$ in normalized weighting schemes, or may represent $1/\sigma^2$ where $\sigma$ is the measurement uncertainty in radians. The weight is dimensionless (unit 1). Related quantities include the measured and reconstructed MSE polarization angles themselves.

name: normalized_poloidal_magnetic_flux_of_minimum_safety_factor
description: Normalized poloidal flux $\psi_N$ at the radial location where the safety factor $q$ reaches its minimum value.
documentation: This scalar identifies the flux-surface position of the minimum safety factor $q_{\min}$ expressed in the normalized poloidal flux coordinate $\psi_N \in [0,1]$, where $\psi_N = 0$ corresponds to the magnetic axis and $\psi_N = 1$ to the last closed flux surface. The safety factor profile $q(\psi_N)$ is generally monotonically increasing in conventional tokamak scenarios, but reversed-shear or advanced-tokamak scenarios develop a non-monotonic $q$ profile with a local minimum at an interior flux surface. The location $\psi_N(q_{\min})$ is a key parameter for MHD stability analysis: internal transport barriers and double tearing modes are often associated with low-order rational surfaces near $q_{\min}$. Equilibrium reconstruction codes compute $q(\psi_N)$ from the magnetic geometry, and the minimum and its radial position are extracted as global scalar outputs. Typical values range from $\psi_N \approx 0.3$ to $0.7$ in reversed-shear plasmas, depending on the current-density profile.

name: normalized_toroidal_flux_coordinate_of_electron_density_constraint
description: Normalized toroidal flux coordinate $\rho_{\mathrm{tor,N}}$ at which an electron density constraint is applied in equilibrium reconstruction.
documentation: This quantity specifies the radial location, expressed as the normalized toroidal flux coordinate $\rho_{\mathrm{tor,N}} = \sqrt{\Phi/\Phi_{\mathrm{LCFS}}}$, where $\Phi$ is the toroidal magnetic flux enclosed by a flux surface and $\Phi_{\mathrm{LCFS}}$ is the value at the last closed flux surface (LCFS). Electron density profile measurements (from Thomson scattering, interferometry, or reflectometry) are mapped onto this coordinate and supplied as constraints to equilibrium or integrated-modeling solvers. By convention, $\rho_{\mathrm{tor,N}} = 0$ corresponds to the magnetic axis and $\rho_{\mathrm{tor,N}} = 1$ to the LCFS (or 99.x% of it for fixed-boundary equilibria). This coordinate is commonly used because it provides a flux-surface label that is monotonic and well-defined across the plasma cross section.

name: normalized_toroidal_flux_coordinate_of_minimum_safety_factor
description: Normalized toroidal flux coordinate $\rho_{\mathrm{tor,N}}$ at the radial position of the minimum safety factor $q_{\min}$.
documentation: This scalar gives the flux-surface location of the minimum safety factor expressed in the normalized toroidal flux coordinate $\rho_{\mathrm{tor,N}} = \sqrt{\Phi/\Phi_\mathrm{edge}}$, where $\Phi$ is the toroidal magnetic flux enclosed by a given flux surface and $\Phi_\mathrm{edge}$ is its value at the last closed flux surface. It ranges from 0 (magnetic axis) to 1 (separatrix). In reversed-shear plasmas typical values lie in the range $\rho_{\mathrm{tor,N}} \approx 0.3$–$0.7$, corresponding to the internal minimum of the $q$ profile. This coordinate is often preferred over $\psi_N$ in transport codes because it provides a more uniform radial grid weighting. Equilibrium solvers compute the full $q(\rho_{\mathrm{tor,N}})$ profile and extract the minimum and its position as global quantities. The radial location of $q_{\min}$ is important for predicting the onset of internal kink modes and double tearing modes, and for designing advanced-tokamak scenarios with internal transport barriers.

name: parallel_component_of_current_density
description: Component of the current density vector projected along the local magnetic field direction.
documentation:
The parallel current density $j_\parallel = \mathbf{j} \cdot \mathbf{B} / |\mathbf{B}|$ represents the projection of the plasma current density vector onto the local magnetic field line direction. It is a fundamental quantity in MHD equilibrium and stability analysis, governing the safety factor profile and the drive for current-driven instabilities such as kink and tearing modes.

In tokamak equilibria, $j_\parallel$ is dominated by the ohmic current, bootstrap current, and any externally driven current (e.g., from neutral beam or RF current drive). Typical peak values are $1$–$10\;\mathrm{MA\,m^{-2}}$ in conventional scenarios. The quantity is provided on a general grid description (GGD) mesh, with one scalar value per grid element. Positive when the current flows in the direction of $\mathbf{B}$.

name: passive_loop_current
description: Electric current flowing in a passive conducting structure (e.g., vacuum vessel segment or stabilization loop) surrounding the plasma.
documentation: Passive loop current $I_{\mathrm{passive}}$ (in amperes) is the current induced in passive conducting structures — such as vacuum vessel segments, stabilization plates, or passive stabilization loops — by time-varying magnetic flux from the plasma and active poloidal field coils. These currents are governed by the circuit equation $L \, dI/dt + R \, I = -d\Phi/dt$, where $L$ is the loop self-inductance (H), $R$ is the loop resistance ($\Omega$), and $\Phi$ is the linked magnetic flux (Wb). Passive currents play a critical role in vertical stability control, acting as a natural slowing mechanism for vertical displacement events (VDEs). They are typically inferred from magnetic measurements (pick-up coils and flux loops) via equilibrium reconstruction or directly measured with Rogowski coils around the vessel segments. In large tokamaks, passive loop currents can reach tens of kA during transient events.

name: passive_loop_current_constraint_weight
description: Dimensionless weight assigned to the passive loop current measurement in the $\chi^2$ cost function of equilibrium reconstruction.
documentation: Standard $\chi^2$ weight controlling the relative importance of the passive loop current measurement in the equilibrium reconstruction. A larger weight increases the influence of this measurement on the reconstructed equilibrium, effectively reducing its allowed residual in the cost function $\chi^2 = \sum_i w_i (y_i^{\mathrm{meas}} - y_i^{\mathrm{recon}})^2$, where $w_i$ is the weight (dimensionless), $y_i^{\mathrm{meas}}$ is the measured value, and $y_i^{\mathrm{recon}}$ is the reconstructed value. Weights are often set proportional to the inverse variance $1/\sigma_i^2$ of the measurement uncertainty. A weight of zero effectively excludes the measurement from the reconstruction. Proper tuning of constraint weights is essential for balancing diagnostic inputs and avoiding over-fitting to noisy signals.

name: plasma_boundary_gap_distance
description: Distance between a fixed reference point and the separatrix along a specified direction in the poloidal plane.
documentation:
The gap value $d_{\mathrm{gap}}$ (in meters) represents the shortest distance measured along a predefined direction from a reference point (typically on the first wall or limiter) to the last closed flux surface (separatrix). It quantifies the clearance between the plasma boundary and plasma-facing components in a given poloidal direction.

Gap distances are fundamental inputs to the plasma shape and position control system. A real-time controller adjusts poloidal field coil currents to maintain all gap values above safe minimum thresholds, preventing unintended plasma-wall contact that could damage components or trigger disruptions. In typical large tokamaks (e.g., ITER), gap values range from a few centimeters at the closest approach to tens of centimeters at well-cleared regions.

Positive when the separatrix is farther from the wall than the reference point direction implies (i.e., plasma is pulled inward from the reference point). Each gap entry is paired with a reference point defined by its major radius and vertical coordinate, as well as an angular direction in the poloidal plane. The gap is undefined when the plasma is limited rather than diverted if the reference direction does not intersect the LCFS.

name: plasma_current_constraint_measurement_time
description: Time instant at which the plasma current measurement was sampled or interpolated for use as an equilibrium reconstruction constraint.
documentation: Records the exact time slice taken from the plasma current ($I_p$, in A) diagnostic time array and supplied as a constraint to the equilibrium solver. When the requested time does not align with an existing sample in the source diagnostic data, linear interpolation is applied between bracketing samples. This metadata ensures full temporal traceability between the equilibrium reconstruction output and the input plasma current measurement. Typical values span from 0 to several seconds, corresponding to the duration of a tokamak discharge. The associated constraint weight and measured/reconstructed values are stored in companion quantities.

name: plasma_filament_current
description: Electric current carried by a discrete plasma filament element used in filamentary representations of the plasma current distribution.
documentation: In filamentary plasma models, the distributed toroidal plasma current is approximated by a set of discrete current-carrying filaments (elements), each located at a specified $(R, Z)$ position in the poloidal cross section. The current $I_k$ in the $k$-th filament, expressed in amperes, contributes to the total poloidal magnetic flux via the Biot–Savart law: $\psi(R,Z) = \sum_k I_k \, G(R,Z; R_k,Z_k)$, where $G$ is the axisymmetric Green's function. This representation is widely used in free-boundary equilibrium codes (e.g., EFIT, CREATE-L) and in plasma response models. Individual filament currents typically range from a few hundred amperes to tens of kiloamperes, depending on the total plasma current and the number of filaments. The sum of all filament currents equals the total toroidal plasma current $I_p$.

name: plasma_inductance
description: Total plasma inductance defined as $L_p = 2 E_{\mathrm{mag}} / I_p^2$, characterizing the magnetic energy stored per unit plasma current squared.
documentation: The plasma inductance $L_p$ (in henries) quantifies the magnetic energy stored by the plasma current distribution via $L_p = 2 E_{\mathrm{mag}} / I_p^2$, where $E_{\mathrm{mag}} = \frac{1}{2} \int \psi \, j_\phi \, dS$ is the poloidal magnetic energy integrated over the plasma cross-section, $\psi$ is the poloidal magnetic flux (Wb), $j_\phi$ the toroidal current density ($\mathrm{A/m^2}$), and $I_p$ the total plasma current (A). This quantity is closely related to the normalized internal inductance $l_i$ via $L_p = \mu_0 R_0 (l_i/2 + \ln(8R_0/a) - 2)$ in a circular-cross-section approximation, where $R_0$ is the major radius, $a$ the minor radius, and $\mu_0$ the vacuum permeability. Typical values in large tokamaks range from $0.5$ to $5\,\mu\mathrm{H}$. Plasma inductance is determined from equilibrium reconstruction codes such as EFIT or LIUQE, and it influences the plasma response to current ramps and disruptions. It plays a key role in the poloidal field circuit design and in estimating the inductive voltage budget during plasma startup and ramp-down.
validation_issues: link_not_found:name:poloidal_magnetic_flux

name: plasma_resistance
description: Effective plasma resistance defined as the ratio of ohmic power dissipation to the square of the plasma current.
documentation:
The plasma resistance $R_p$ is defined by $R_p = \int \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{j}\, dV \,/\, I_p^2$, where $\mathbf{E}$ is the electric field (V m$^{-1}$), $\mathbf{j}$ is the current density (A m$^{-2}$), the integral is over the plasma volume, and $I_p$ is the total plasma current (A). This definition generalizes Ohm's law to a toroidal geometry with non-uniform resistivity.

Plasma resistance is a global equilibrium quantity that characterizes ohmic dissipation and is closely related to the effective charge $Z_\mathrm{eff}$ and electron temperature through the Spitzer resistivity $\eta \propto Z_\mathrm{eff}} T_e^{-3/2}$. Typical values in medium-to-large tokamaks range from $1$ to $100\;\mu\Omega$, decreasing with higher electron temperature. The quantity is obtained from equilibrium reconstruction or from direct measurement of loop voltage and plasma current. It is expressed in ohms.

name: poloidal_angle
description:
Angle in the poloidal plane centered on the magnetic axis, oriented so that $(
ablaho_{\mathrm{tor}},\, 
abla	heta,\, 
abla\phi)$ forms a right-handed set.
documentation:
The poloidal angle $	heta$ (in rad) is a coordinate defined in the poloidal cross-section of a toroidal plasma, measured from the outboard midplane around the magnetic axis. Its orientation is chosen such that the triplet $(
abla ho_{\mathrm{tor,n}},\, 
abla 	heta,\, 
abla \phi)$ constitutes a right-handed coordinate system, where $ho_{\mathrm{tor,n}}$ is the normalized toroidal flux coordinate and $\phi$ is the toroidal angle. Under COCOS conventions the sign of $	heta$ is linked to the signs of $I_p$ and $B_\phi$; the specific convention used must match the declared COCOS index. In a 2-D equilibrium grid, $	heta$ provides one axis (with $ho$ or $\psi$ as the other) on which quantities such as the poloidal magnetic flux, current density, and pressure are discretized. Values range from $0$ to $2\pi$ (or equivalently $-\pi$ to $\pi$). Straight-field-line variants of this angle (e.g., equal-arc or Boozer) are commonly used in MHD stability and turbulence codes to simplify the field-line-following representation.

name: poloidal_magnetic_magnetic_field_probe_voltage
description: Measured poloidal magnetic field value from a magnetic field probe, used as a constraint in equilibrium reconstruction.
documentation: This quantity represents the measured poloidal magnetic field $B_\theta$ (in T) as recorded by a magnetic field probe (often a pickup coil oriented to sense the poloidal component). Although the name includes 'voltage', the stored value is the calibrated magnetic field in tesla, converted from the raw induced voltage $V = -N A \, dB/dt$ (where $N$ is the coil turn count and $A$ is the effective coil area in m$^2$) via time integration and calibration factors. These measurements serve as constraint inputs in Grad–Shafranov equilibrium solvers: the reconstructed equilibrium must reproduce the measured $B_\theta$ within the assigned $\chi^2$ weight tolerance. Typical poloidal probe signals at the vessel wall of a medium-sized tokamak lie in the range $0.01$–$0.5$ T. Probe arrays are usually distributed around the poloidal circumference to provide sufficient spatial coverage for accurate reconstruction.
validation_issues: link_not_found:name:poloidal_component_of_magnetic_field

name: pressure_constraint_weight
description: Dimensionless weight assigned to a pressure measurement in the equilibrium reconstruction χ² minimization.
documentation: Standard χ² weight controlling the relative importance of a pressure measurement in the equilibrium reconstruction. In the cost function $\chi^2 = \sum_i w_i (p_i^{\mathrm{meas}} - p_i^{\mathrm{recon}})^2$, the weight $w_i$ scales the residual contribution of each pressure data point (e.g., from Thomson scattering or charge-exchange recombination spectroscopy). Larger weights force the solver to fit that pressure measurement more tightly, which directly constrains the pressure profile $p(\psi)$ entering the Grad-Shafranov equation $\Delta^* \psi = -\mu_0 R^2 p'(\psi) - F F'(\psi)$. Values are typically set proportional to $1/\sigma_i^2$ where $\sigma_i$ is the measurement uncertainty, yielding dimensionless numbers whose magnitude depends on the normalization convention of the solver (commonly $10^0$–$10^6$).
validation_issues: link_not_found:name:faraday_angle_constraint_weight

name: rotational_pressure
description: Effective pressure contribution arising from bulk plasma toroidal rotation, supplementing the kinetic pressure in rotating equilibria.
documentation:
The rotational pressure $p_\mathrm{rot}$ accounts for the centrifugal effects of bulk toroidal plasma rotation on the force balance. In a rotating plasma the Grad–Shafranov equation is modified: the total effective pressure becomes $p_\mathrm{eff} = p_\mathrm{kin} + p_\mathrm{rot}$, where the rotational contribution is approximately $p_\mathrm{rot} = \frac{1}{2} \rho \omega_\phi^2 (R^2 - R_0^2)$, with $\rho$ the mass density (kg m$^{-3}$), $\omega_\phi$ the toroidal angular frequency (rad s$^{-1}$), $R$ the major radius (m), and $R_0$ a reference major radius.

Rotational pressure is significant in neutral-beam-heated plasmas with strong toroidal rotation, where $\omega_\phi$ can reach $10^5$ rad s$^{-1}$ and $p_\mathrm{rot}$ can be a non-negligible fraction of $p_\mathrm{kin}$, typically up to 10–20 % in high-performance discharges. It is obtained from charge-exchange recombination spectroscopy measurements of the toroidal rotation velocity combined with density profiles. The unit is Pa.

name: rotational_pressure_constraint_weight
description: Dimensionless weight assigned to the rotational pressure measurement in the equilibrium reconstruction χ² cost function.
documentation:
Standard χ² weight controlling the relative importance of the rotational pressure measurement in the equilibrium reconstruction. The rotational pressure captures centrifugal effects of bulk toroidal rotation and modifies the force balance in the Grad–Shafranov equation. A larger weight forces the reconstructed equilibrium to match the measured rotational pressure profile more closely.

Typical values range from 0 (measurement ignored) upward; in inverse-variance weighting schemes the weight corresponds to $1/\sigma^2$ where $\sigma$ is the measurement uncertainty in Pa. The weight is dimensionless (unit 1).

name: safety_factor_at_magnetic_axis
description: Value of the MHD safety factor $q$ evaluated at the magnetic axis, characterizing the on-axis field-line pitch.
documentation: The safety factor at the magnetic axis, $q_0 = q(\rho=0)$, quantifies the number of toroidal transits a magnetic field line completes per single poloidal transit in the limit approaching the magnetic axis. It is defined as $q_0 = \lim_{\rho\to 0} \frac{d\Phi}{d\psi}$, where $\Phi$ is the toroidal magnetic flux and $\psi$ is the poloidal magnetic flux. For a standard tokamak, $q_0$ is typically close to or slightly above unity ($q_0 \approx 0.8$–$1.2$) in sawtoothing discharges, dropping below 1 just before a sawtooth crash. In advanced tokamak scenarios with reversed shear, $q_0$ can be significantly above 1. The on-axis safety factor is a key parameter for MHD stability analysis: internal kink modes ($m=1$, $n=1$) are destabilized when $q_0 < 1$. It is typically obtained from equilibrium reconstruction codes (e.g., EFIT, LIUQE) constrained by magnetic and kinetic measurements, or from motional Stark effect (MSE) current profile diagnostics. Positive when the toroidal and poloidal fields have the standard relative orientation under COCOS conventions.
validation_issues: link_not_found:name:toroidal_magnetic_flux

name: toroidal_component_of_magnetic_field_of_magnetic_axis
description: Total toroidal magnetic field $B_\phi$ evaluated at the magnetic axis of the plasma equilibrium.
documentation: The toroidal component of the magnetic field at the magnetic axis, $B_{\phi,0}$, is a fundamental scalar characterizing the equilibrium. It combines the vacuum toroidal field produced by the toroidal field coils and the paramagnetic/diamagnetic contribution from the plasma current. At the magnetic axis located at major radius $R_0$, the vacuum contribution follows $B_{\phi,\mathrm{vac}} = B_0 R_{\mathrm{ref}} / R_0$, where $B_0$ is the reference vacuum field at radius $R_{\mathrm{ref}}$. Typical values range from 1–6 T in present-day tokamaks and up to ~5.3 T in ITER. This quantity is used to normalize many dimensionless plasma parameters, including $\beta_t = 2\mu_0 \langle p \rangle / B_{\phi,0}^2$. Positive when $B_\phi$ is in the $+\phi$ direction under the chosen COCOS convention.

name: toroidal_current_density_constraint_measurement_time
description: Time instant at which the toroidal current density measurement was sampled or interpolated for use as an equilibrium reconstruction constraint.
documentation: Specifies the exact time slice extracted from the toroidal current density ($j_\phi$, in A·m$^{-2}$) diagnostic time array and supplied as a constraint in the Grad–Shafranov equilibrium reconstruction. If the desired time does not coincide with an existing sample in the source data, linear interpolation between neighboring samples is used. This timestamp ensures reproducibility and traceability of the constraint dataset. Toroidal current density profiles are typically obtained from motional Stark effect (MSE) diagnostics or polarimetry, with measurement times spanning the flat-top and ramp phases of a discharge. Standard χ² weight controlling the relative importance of this measurement in the equilibrium reconstruction is handled by a separate weight quantity.

name: triangularity_of_plasma_boundary
description: Average triangularity of the last closed flux surface, quantifying the D-shaped deviation of the plasma cross section from an ellipse.
documentation: Triangularity $\delta$ measures how much the plasma boundary is pointed (D-shaped) compared to a pure ellipse. It is defined as $\delta = (R_{\mathrm{geo}} - R_{\mathrm{X}}) / a$, where $R_{\mathrm{geo}}$ is the geometric major radius of the boundary, $R_{\mathrm{X}}$ is the major radius of the point of maximum vertical extent (upper or lower), and $a$ is the minor radius. The reported value is typically the average of the upper and lower triangularities: $\delta = (\delta_{\mathrm{upper}} + \delta_{\mathrm{lower}}) / 2$. Positive triangularity ($\delta > 0$) corresponds to the conventional D-shape where the plasma is narrower on the high-field side; negative triangularity ($\delta < 0$) is an inverted D-shape being explored for its favorable stability and confinement properties. Triangularity is a key shaping parameter affecting MHD stability (ballooning, kink modes), energy confinement scaling, and edge localized mode (ELM) behavior. Typical values in conventional H-mode tokamaks range from $0.2$ to $0.5$; negative-triangularity experiments operate near $-0.2$ to $-0.4$. The quantity is obtained from equilibrium reconstruction codes (EFIT, LIUQE) or directly from plasma boundary diagnostics. Triangularity is dimensionless and COCOS-independent.

name: upper_triangularity
description: Dimensionless shape parameter quantifying the inward triangular distortion of the upper half of a flux surface relative to the magnetic axis.
documentation: Upper triangularity $\delta_u$ measures how far the uppermost point of a flux surface is shifted inward (toward the high-field side) relative to the geometric center, normalized by the minor radius: $\delta_u = (R_{\mathrm{geo}} - R_{\mathrm{upper}}) / a$, where $R_{\mathrm{geo}}$ is the major radius of the geometric center, $R_{\mathrm{upper}}$ is the major radius of the highest point on the flux surface, and $a$ is the minor radius. Positive values indicate inward shift (conventional positive triangularity), while negative values correspond to negative triangularity configurations that have attracted interest for improved confinement and reduced ELM activity. In conventional H-mode tokamak plasmas, $\delta_u$ typically ranges from 0.1 to 0.5. Upper and lower triangularity are often different due to up-down asymmetry introduced by the divertor configuration and shaping coils. This quantity is a radial profile, evaluated on each flux surface from equilibrium reconstruction codes. Related shape descriptors include elongation and squareness.

name: vacuum_toroidal_field_function
description: Product $R \, B_{\phi,\mathrm{vac}}$ of major radius and vacuum toroidal magnetic field, a conserved quantity in the absence of plasma currents.
documentation: The vacuum toroidal field function $F_\mathrm{vac} = R \, B_{\phi,\mathrm{vac}}$ (in m·T) encapsulates the $1/R$ dependence of the vacuum toroidal field produced by the toroidal field coils. In vacuum (no toroidal plasma current), $F_\mathrm{vac}$ is spatially constant and equals $R_0 B_0$, where $R_0$ is the reference major radius and $B_0$ is the toroidal field at $R_0$. When plasma is present, the full poloidal-flux-dependent function $F(\psi) = R B_\phi$ deviates from $F_\mathrm{vac}$ due to diamagnetic and paramagnetic toroidal currents. The measured value of $F_\mathrm{vac}$ is typically inferred from diamagnetic loop measurements or the known coil current and geometry. For large tokamaks (e.g., ITER at $B_0 = 5.3$ T, $R_0 = 6.2$ m), $F_\mathrm{vac} \approx 33$ m·T. This quantity is a key constraint in Grad–Shafranov equilibrium reconstruction, anchoring the vacuum contribution to the toroidal field profile.

name: vertical_coordinate_of_electron_density_constraint
description: Vertical (Z) position of the measurement point where an electron density constraint is applied in equilibrium reconstruction.
documentation: The vertical coordinate $Z$ (in meters) specifies the height in the poloidal cross-section of the tokamak where an electron density measurement is used as a constraint in equilibrium reconstruction. Electron density constraints are typically derived from diagnostics such as Thomson scattering or interferometry, and their spatial positions must be recorded so the reconstruction code can map them onto the computational grid. The $Z$ coordinate is defined in a right-handed cylindrical coordinate system $(R, \phi, Z)$, where $Z = 0$ corresponds to the device midplane. Typical values range from approximately $-2\,\mathrm{m}$ to $+2\,\mathrm{m}$ depending on machine size. Standard $\chi^2$ weight controlling the relative importance of this measurement in the equilibrium reconstruction is handled separately.

name: vertical_coordinate_of_ggd_grid_point
description: Vertical (Z) position of a node or element in the generalized grid description (GGD) used for equilibrium representation.
documentation: The vertical coordinate of a GGD grid point gives the $Z$-position (in meters) of a geometric element (node, edge, or cell centroid) within the generalized grid description framework. GGD grids provide a flexible unstructured-mesh representation of the poloidal cross-section for equilibrium and transport quantities that cannot be conveniently mapped onto structured $(R,Z)$ or flux-coordinate grids. Each grid point's vertical coordinate, together with its radial coordinate $R$, fully specifies the location in the poloidal plane under the assumption of toroidal axisymmetry. The range of $Z$ values spans the computational domain, typically from the bottom to the top of the vacuum vessel (e.g., $-2$ m to $+2$ m for large tokamaks like ITER). These coordinates are essential for interpolating field quantities such as the magnetic field and pressure onto the unstructured mesh.

name: vertical_coordinate_of_measured_strike_point
description: Measured vertical (Z) position of a divertor strike point used as a constraint in equilibrium reconstruction.
documentation: The vertical coordinate $Z$ (in meters) of the experimentally measured strike point, i.e., the location where a separatrix leg intersects a divertor target plate. Strike point positions are typically measured using infrared thermography, Langmuir probe arrays embedded in the divertor tiles, or visible camera imaging. In the cylindrical coordinate system $(R, \phi, Z)$, the $Z$ value of the strike point is generally close to the divertor tile surface elevation. For a lower single-null configuration in a large tokamak, typical values are $Z \approx -1.0$ to $-1.8\,\mathrm{m}$; for upper single-null, correspondingly positive values. The measured strike point position is compared against the reconstructed separatrix to assess equilibrium quality and to constrain the reconstruction.

name: vertical_coordinate_of_measured_x_point
description: Measured vertical (Z) position of an X-point used as a constraint in equilibrium reconstruction.
documentation: The vertical coordinate $Z$ (in meters) of the experimentally determined magnetic X-point (magnetic field null), where the poloidal magnetic field vanishes: $B_\theta(R_X, Z_X) = 0$. X-point positions are inferred from magnetic probe arrays combined with equilibrium reconstruction codes, or from dedicated X-point diagnostics. In a typical lower single-null tokamak configuration, the X-point lies at $Z \approx -0.8$ to $-1.5\,\mathrm{m}$ depending on machine size and plasma shape. The measured X-point location constrains the separatrix geometry in the equilibrium solver, directly affecting the determination of the last closed flux surface (LCFS). Accurate X-point positioning is critical for divertor performance prediction and scrape-off layer modeling.

name: vertical_coordinate_of_parallel_current_density_constraint
description: Vertical position (Z coordinate) at which the parallel current density constraint is applied in equilibrium reconstruction.
documentation: This quantity specifies the vertical coordinate $Z$ in meters of the spatial location where a measurement or estimate of the parallel current density $j_\parallel$ is used as a constraint in the equilibrium reconstruction inverse problem. Standard χ² weight controlling the relative importance of this measurement in the equilibrium reconstruction. The parallel current density $j_\parallel = \mathbf{j} \cdot \mathbf{B}/|\mathbf{B}|$ (in A/m²) is a key input for constraining the internal current profile, often derived from motional Stark effect (MSE) diagnostics or polarimetry. The $Z$ position, together with the corresponding major radius, identifies the measurement point in the poloidal cross section. Values span the full vertical extent of the plasma, typically $|Z| < a \kappa$ where $a$ is the minor radius and $\kappa$ the elongation.

name: vertical_coordinate_of_plasma_boundary_gap_reference_point
description: Vertical ($Z$) coordinate of the reference point from which a plasma-wall gap distance is measured.
documentation: Specifies the height $Z$ (in m) of a fixed reference point used to define a gap distance between the plasma boundary and a first-wall or limiter structure. Gap reference points are placed at critical locations—typically near the inboard midplane, outboard midplane, upper, and lower divertor regions—where proximity of the plasma boundary to material surfaces must be monitored for wall protection and shape control. Together with the corresponding radial coordinate ($R$) and the gap direction angle, this $Z$ value fully defines the origin and direction of the gap measurement. Equilibrium reconstruction codes such as EFIT or real-time shape controllers compute the gap as the distance from this reference point to the nearest point on the last closed flux surface (LCFS) along the specified direction. Typical values range over the full vertical extent of the vacuum vessel, e.g., $-2$ to $+2$ m for a large tokamak.

name: vertical_coordinate_of_pressure_constraint
description: Vertical (Z) position of the measurement point where a pressure constraint is applied in equilibrium reconstruction.
documentation: The vertical coordinate $Z$ (in meters) specifies the height in the poloidal cross-section where a total plasma pressure measurement constrains the equilibrium reconstruction. Pressure constraints typically originate from Thomson scattering, charge-exchange recombination spectroscopy, or other profile diagnostics that provide local kinetic pressure $p = n_e k_B T_e + n_i k_B T_i$, where $n_e$ is the electron number density, $T_e$ the electron temperature, $n_i$ the ion number density, and $T_i$ the ion temperature. The coordinate is defined in the standard right-handed cylindrical system $(R, \phi, Z)$ with $Z = 0$ at the device midplane. Typical values span from $-2\,\mathrm{m}$ to $+2\,\mathrm{m}$ for large tokamaks. This position is used internally by equilibrium solvers to locate the constraint on the poloidal grid.

name: vertical_coordinate_of_rotational_pressure_constraint
description: Vertical (Z) position where a rotational pressure constraint is applied in equilibrium reconstruction.
documentation: The vertical coordinate $Z$ (in meters) specifies the height in the poloidal cross-section where a rotational (centrifugal) pressure measurement constrains the equilibrium solution. In rotating plasmas, the total pressure includes a centrifugal contribution $p_{\mathrm{rot}} = \frac{1}{2} m_i n_i R^2 \omega^2$, where $m_i$ is the ion mass, $n_i$ the ion density, $R$ the major radius, and $\omega$ the toroidal angular velocity. Diagnostics such as charge-exchange recombination spectroscopy provide rotational velocity profiles from which the rotational pressure can be derived. The coordinate follows the standard cylindrical system $(R, \phi, Z)$ with $Z = 0$ at the midplane. Typical values range from approximately $-2\,\mathrm{m}$ to $+2\,\mathrm{m}$ depending on machine geometry.

name: vertical_coordinate_of_safety_factor_constraint
description: Vertical (Z) position where a safety factor constraint is applied in equilibrium reconstruction.
documentation: The vertical coordinate $Z$ (in meters) specifies the height in the poloidal cross-section where a safety factor $q$ measurement constrains the magnetic equilibrium reconstruction. The safety factor $q = \frac{d\Phi}{d\psi}$ (ratio of toroidal to poloidal magnetic flux increments) is often constrained using motional Stark effect (MSE) polarimetry or MHD mode analysis that localizes rational surfaces. The coordinate is defined in the cylindrical system $(R, \phi, Z)$ with $Z = 0$ at the midplane. Typical values span from $-2\,\mathrm{m}$ to $+2\,\mathrm{m}$ for large devices. These spatial positions allow the equilibrium solver to match measured $q$ values to the correct flux surfaces in the reconstruction.

The $Z$ coordinate, paired with the corresponding major radius $R$, specifies the constraint point in the poloidal plane. Values span the plasma vertical extent, typically $|Z| < a \kappa$.

name: elongation_of_plasma_boundary
description: Ratio of the vertical half-height to the horizontal half-width of the plasma boundary, quantifying the vertical stretching of the plasma cross-section.
documentation: Elongation $\kappa$ of the plasma boundary is defined as $\kappa = b / a$, where $b$ is the vertical half-height (distance from the geometric axis to the top or bottom extremity of the boundary) and $a$ is the horizontal minor radius. A circular cross-section has $\kappa = 1$; modern diverted tokamaks typically operate with $\kappa \approx 1.6$–$1.9$ to increase the plasma current for a given edge safety factor $q_{95}$, thereby improving confinement and MHD stability. ITER targets $\kappa \approx 1.7$ at the separatrix. Elongation is obtained from equilibrium reconstruction codes (EFIT, LIUQE, NICE) or direct analysis of the boundary $(R, Z)$ contour. Higher elongation increases the vertical instability growth rate, requiring active feedback stabilization. Upper and lower half-elongations may differ in up-down asymmetric configurations.

name: lower_triangularity_of_plasma_boundary
description: Lower triangularity of the plasma boundary, measuring the D-shaped distortion of the lower half of the plasma cross section.
documentation: The lower triangularity $\delta_{\mathrm{lower}}$ is defined as $\delta_{\mathrm{lower}} = (R_{\mathrm{geo}} - R_{\mathrm{Z,min}}) / a$, where $R_{\mathrm{geo}}$ is the geometric major radius of the boundary, $R_{\mathrm{Z,min}}$ is the major radius at the point of minimum $Z$ (the lowest point of the boundary), and $a$ is the minor radius. This parameter specifically characterizes the shape of the lower portion of the plasma cross section and can differ significantly from the upper triangularity in single-null divertor configurations, where the X-point geometry pulls the lower boundary into a more pointed shape. In lower-single-null (LSN) diverted plasmas, $\delta_{\mathrm{lower}}$ is generally larger than $\delta_{\mathrm{upper}}$ because the X-point is at the bottom. Typical values range from $0.2$ to $0.6$ in conventional H-mode, though they can exceed $0.7$ in strongly shaped configurations. The average triangularity is $\delta = (\delta_{\mathrm{upper}} + \delta_{\mathrm{lower}})/2$. Lower triangularity affects the lower divertor heat flux distribution and the stability of edge localized modes. It is obtained from equilibrium reconstruction codes and is dimensionless and COCOS-independent.

name: major_radius_of_x_point
description: Major radius $R$ of a magnetic X-point (field null) in the poloidal plane of a diverted plasma equilibrium.
documentation: The major radius $R_X$ (in meters) locates the radial position of a magnetic X-point, where the poloidal magnetic field vanishes ($B_p = 0$) and the poloidal flux $\psi$ exhibits a saddle-point topology. X-points define the separatrix that divides closed and open field-line regions and determine the divertor geometry. Most diverted tokamak equilibria have a primary (lower or upper) X-point and may feature a secondary X-point for double-null configurations. Equilibrium reconstruction codes identify X-points by solving $\partial\psi/\partial R = 0$ and $\partial\psi/\partial Z = 0$ simultaneously. Typical values of $R_X$ lie between the magnetic axis and the outer wall; for ITER the primary X-point is near $R \approx 5.5$–$6.0$ m, while for JET $R_X \approx 2.6$–$2.9$ m. The associated vertical position completes the X-point location in the $(R, Z)$ plane.

name: plasma_boundary_distance_of_closest_wall_point
description: Minimum distance from the plasma boundary (last closed flux surface) to the closest point on the first wall.
documentation: This quantity gives the shortest Euclidean distance $d_{\mathrm{min}}$ between the plasma boundary (last closed flux surface, LCFS) and the nearest point on the physical first wall. It is a key engineering safety parameter: small values indicate that the plasma is close to making uncontrolled contact with the wall, risking localized heat loads and impurity influx. In diverted configurations the closest wall point is typically near the divertor targets or baffles, while in limited configurations it coincides with the limiter contact point where $d_{\mathrm{min}} \to 0$. The value is computed by equilibrium reconstruction codes (EFIT, LIUQE) or real-time shape controllers by evaluating the distance from the reconstructed LCFS to a discretized wall contour. Typical values range from a few millimeters during high-elongation operation to tens of centimeters in conservative scenarios. A value of zero implies wall contact. The associated wall-point location is described by `major_radius_of_closest_wall_point` and the corresponding vertical position.

name: upper_triangularity_of_plasma_boundary
description: Upper triangularity of the plasma boundary, measuring the D-shaped distortion of the upper half of the plasma cross section.
documentation: The upper triangularity $\delta_{\mathrm{upper}}$ is defined as $\delta_{\mathrm{upper}} = (R_{\mathrm{geo}} - R_{\mathrm{Z,max}}) / a$, where $R_{\mathrm{geo}}$ is the geometric major radius of the boundary, $R_{\mathrm{Z,max}}$ is the major radius at the point of maximum $Z$ (the highest point of the boundary), and $a$ is the minor radius. This parameter characterizes the shaping of the upper portion of the plasma cross section. In upper-single-null (USN) configurations, $\delta_{\mathrm{upper}}$ tends to be larger than $\delta_{\mathrm{lower}}$ due to the proximity of the upper X-point. In double-null configurations, upper and lower triangularities are approximately equal. Typical values range from $0.1$ to $0.5$ in conventional scenarios. The average triangularity relates as $\delta = (\delta_{\mathrm{upper}} + \delta_{\mathrm{lower}})/2$. Upper triangularity affects the upper divertor heat flux footprint and the stability boundaries of peeling-ballooning modes that drive ELMs. It is extracted from equilibrium reconstruction codes (EFIT, LIUQE) and is dimensionless and COCOS-independent.

name: vertical_coordinate_of_geometric_axis
description: Vertical (Z) position of the geometric center of the plasma boundary cross-section in the poloidal plane.
documentation: The vertical coordinate of the geometric axis is defined as the Z-coordinate of the centroid of the last closed flux surface (LCFS) boundary in the poloidal $(R,Z)$ plane. It is computed as $Z_{\mathrm{geo}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} Z_i$, where $Z_i$ are the vertical positions of boundary points and $N$ is the number of points. This quantity is distinct from the magnetic axis vertical position, which is the location of the flux-surface degenerate point where $|\nabla \psi|=0$. Equilibrium reconstruction codes such as EFIT or LIUQE compute the geometric axis from the plasma boundary shape. In tokamaks, the geometric axis vertical coordinate is typically close to the device midplane ($Z \approx 0$ m) but shifts vertically during vertical displacement events or shaped plasma operation. Typical values range from $-0.5$ m to $+0.5$ m relative to the machine midplane, depending on plasma shaping and vertical position control.

name: vertical_coordinate_of_strike_point
description: Vertical position $Z$ (in m) of a divertor strike point where the separatrix intersects a material surface.
documentation: The vertical coordinate of a strike point specifies the height $Z$ (in m, relative to the machine midplane) at which the magnetic separatrix intercepts a plasma-facing component, typically a divertor target plate. Strike-point positions are determined by equilibrium reconstruction or real-time magnetic control algorithms that trace the separatrix field lines to the wall. In a standard lower-single-null diverted plasma the inner strike point is typically at $Z \approx -1.0$ to $-1.8$ m for ITER-class devices, while the outer strike point sits at a similar or slightly different vertical level depending on divertor geometry. During sweeping or detachment experiments, the strike-point $Z$ may shift by tens of centimeters on sub-second timescales. Monitoring this coordinate is critical for divertor heat-flux management and erosion control. Infrared thermography and Langmuir probe arrays embedded in the divertor tiles provide independent measurements of the strike-point location.

name: vertical_coordinate_of_x_point
description: Vertical position (height) of a magnetic X-point in the poloidal cross-section of the equilibrium.
documentation:
The vertical coordinate $Z$ (in meters) of a magnetic X-point, where the poloidal magnetic field vanishes ($B_p = 0$) and the flux surface topology changes from closed to open. X-points are critical topological features in diverted tokamak equilibria that define the separatrix geometry and divertor strike-point locations.

In single-null configurations one X-point is active; in double-null configurations two X-points exist, one above and one below the midplane. The vertical position of the X-point is determined by equilibrium reconstruction codes (e.g., EFIT, LIUQE) from magnetic diagnostic measurements. In medium-sized tokamaks the active X-point typically lies at $|Z| \approx 0.5$–$1.5$ m relative to the magnetic axis.

The sign of $Z$ follows the machine coordinate convention: positive above the midplane, negative below. Real-time knowledge of the X-point position is essential for divertor heat-flux control and disruption avoidance.

name: radial_component_of_magnetic_field
description: Radial ($R$) component of the magnetic field in the cylindrical coordinate system of the tokamak.
documentation: The radial component of the magnetic field, $B_R$, is the projection of the total magnetic field vector $\mathbf{B}$ onto the major-radius direction $\hat{R}$ in the cylindrical coordinate system $(R, \phi, Z)$. In an axisymmetric equilibrium, $B_R$ arises from the poloidal flux function $\psi$ via $B_R = -\frac{1}{R}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial Z}$, where $\psi$ is the poloidal magnetic flux (in Wb/rad) and $R$ is the major radius in meters. This component is typically obtained from equilibrium reconstruction codes (e.g., EFIT, LIUQE) that solve the Grad–Shafranov equation, or from direct magnetic probe measurements projected onto $\hat{R}$. In a well-centered tokamak equilibrium, $B_R$ is much smaller than $B_\phi$ (typically $|B_R| \sim 0.01$–$0.1$ T compared to $|B_\phi| \sim 1$–$5$ T), and it vanishes on the outboard midplane for an up-down symmetric equilibrium. The sign of $B_R$ depends on the local poloidal field topology and the COCOS convention adopted for $\psi$.

name: contravariant_metric_tensor
description: Contravariant metric tensor $g^{ij}$ characterizing the local geometry of the curvilinear coordinate system used in equilibrium reconstruction.
documentation: The contravariant metric tensor $g^{ij}$ encodes how infinitesimal coordinate displacements map to physical distances in a curvilinear coordinate system $(\psi, \theta, \phi)$ commonly used in tokamak equilibrium descriptions. Its components appear in the flux-coordinate representation of differential operators such as $\nabla$ and $\nabla^2$, which are essential for solving the Grad-Shafranov equation and evaluating transport coefficients. The tensor is symmetric, so only six independent components exist: $g^{11}$, $g^{12}$, $g^{13}$, $g^{22}$, $g^{23}$, and $g^{33}$. It is the matrix inverse of the covariant metric tensor $g_{ij}$, satisfying $g^{ik} g_{kj} = \delta^i_j$. These components are computed on the equilibrium coordinate grid by equilibrium reconstruction codes (e.g., EFIT, LIUQE, NICE). The tensor is dimensionless (unit 1) because it relates coordinate increments that may themselves carry mixed dimensions. Typical values depend strongly on the coordinate system choice and radial location; near the magnetic axis $g^{11}$ can be of order $1/a^2$ where $a$ is the minor radius in meters.
