name: inner_triangularity_of_plasma_boundary
description: Inner (high-field-side) triangularity of the plasma boundary, measuring the inward horizontal displacement of the vertical extrema relative to the geometric center.
documentation: The inner triangularity $\delta_{\mathrm{inner}}$ quantifies the D-shape distortion of the plasma boundary as seen from the high-field side. While the standard triangularity uses the horizontal offset of the point of maximum $|Z|$ relative to the geometric center, the inner triangularity specifically characterizes the shape of the inboard portion of the boundary contour. It is defined analogously to the overall triangularity but focuses on the curvature and displacement of the inner half of the cross section. This distinction is important because the inner and outer boundaries can have markedly different curvatures, especially in strongly shaped plasmas or those with single-null divertor configurations. The inner triangularity affects the stability of high-field-side ballooning modes and influences the pedestal structure. In conventional H-mode tokamaks, inner triangularity values typically lie in the range $0.1$–$0.6$, though negative values are possible in negative-triangularity scenarios. The quantity is extracted from equilibrium reconstruction (EFIT, LIUQE, CHEASE) by analyzing the parametric decomposition of the boundary shape. It is dimensionless and COCOS-independent.

name: lower_elongation_of_plasma_boundary
description: Elongation of the lower half of the plasma boundary cross-section, measured with respect to the geometric axis.
documentation: Lower elongation $\kappa_{\mathrm{low}}$ quantifies the vertical stretching of the lower half of the plasma boundary: $\kappa_{\mathrm{low}} = |Z_{\mathrm{geo}} - Z_{\mathrm{lower}}| / a$, where $Z_{\mathrm{geo}}$ is the vertical position of the geometric axis, $Z_{\mathrm{lower}}$ is the $Z$ coordinate of the lowest boundary point, and $a$ is the horizontal minor radius. In up-down symmetric plasmas, $\kappa_{\mathrm{low}} = \kappa_{\mathrm{up}}$; however, single-null divertor configurations (especially lower single null) typically produce $\kappa_{\mathrm{low}} > \kappa_{\mathrm{up}}$ due to the X-point geometry pulling the lower boundary downward. Typical values range from 1.4 to 2.2 depending on the device and shaping capability. This quantity is determined from the plasma boundary contour produced by equilibrium reconstruction. The sum of upper and lower half-elongations, weighted appropriately, recovers the full-boundary elongation.

name: upper_elongation_of_plasma_boundary
description: Elongation of the upper half of the plasma boundary cross-section, measured with respect to the geometric axis.
documentation: Upper elongation $\kappa_{\mathrm{up}}$ quantifies the vertical stretching of the upper half of the plasma boundary: $\kappa_{\mathrm{up}} = |Z_{\mathrm{upper}} - Z_{\mathrm{geo}}| / a$, where $Z_{\mathrm{upper}}$ is the $Z$ coordinate of the highest boundary point, $Z_{\mathrm{geo}}$ is the vertical position of the geometric axis, and $a$ is the horizontal minor radius. In upper single-null configurations, $\kappa_{\mathrm{up}}$ tends to exceed $\kappa_{\mathrm{low}}$ because the upper X-point elongates the boundary upward. Typical values range from 1.3 to 2.1 depending on the shaping coil set and plasma scenario. This parameter is extracted from the boundary contour provided by equilibrium reconstruction codes and is important for assessing vertical stability margins and divertor heat-flux sharing. Combined with the lower elongation, the full boundary elongation and up-down asymmetry can be characterized.

name: toroidal_component_of_current_density
description: Toroidal component of the plasma current density vector $j_\phi$, representing the current per unit area flowing in the toroidal direction.
documentation: The toroidal current density $j_\phi$ (in A·m$^{-2}$) is the component of the plasma current density vector in the toroidal ($\phi$) direction. In axisymmetric equilibrium it is related to the poloidal magnetic flux function $\psi$ through the Grad–Shafranov equation: $$R\,\mu_0 j_\phi = -R\,\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial R^2} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial Z^2} + \frac{1}{R}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial R}$$ (simplified form), where $R$ (m) is the major radius, $Z$ (m) is the vertical coordinate, and $\mu_0 = 4\pi\times10^{-7}$ H/m is the vacuum permeability. The toroidal current density is obtained from equilibrium reconstruction codes and is crucial for determining the safety factor profile $q(\rho)$, magnetic shear, and overall plasma stability. In the core of large tokamaks, $|j_\phi|$ can reach $10^6$–$10^7$ A·m$^{-2}$, peaking on axis in standard monotonic-$q$ scenarios. The sign of $j_\phi$ depends on the toroidal coordinate convention; under COCOS-11, positive $j_\phi$ flows in the $+\phi$ direction. Toroidal current density is related to the total plasma current through the surface integral $I_p = \int j_\phi \, dA$.

name: normalized_toroidal_beta
description: Normalized toroidal beta ($\beta_N$), a key MHD stability figure of merit relating plasma pressure confinement capability to the Troyon limit.
documentation: Normalized toroidal beta is defined as $\beta_N = 100 \, \beta_t \, a \, B_0 / I_p$, where $\beta_t$ is the toroidal beta (ratio of volume-averaged plasma pressure to toroidal magnetic field pressure), $a$ is the plasma minor radius in m, $B_0$ is the vacuum toroidal magnetic field on axis in T, and $I_p$ is the plasma current in MA. The factor of 100 is conventional so that $\beta_N$ is of order unity. The Troyon stability limit predicts ideal MHD instability onset at $\beta_N \approx 2.8$–$3.5$ depending on plasma shape and profiles, making $\beta_N$ a central performance metric for tokamak operation. In high-confinement-mode (H-mode) plasmas, typical values are $\beta_N \sim 1.5$–$3.0$; advanced tokamak scenarios target $\beta_N > 3$. It is computed from equilibrium reconstruction output combining pressure profile integration and global plasma parameters.

name: plasma_internal_inductance
description: Normalized internal inductance $l_i$ (li_3 definition) quantifying the peakedness of the poloidal magnetic field distribution inside the plasma.
documentation: The plasma internal inductance in the li_3 convention is defined as $l_i = \frac{2}{R_0 \mu_0^2 I_p^2} \int B_p^2 \, dV$, where $R_0$ is the geometric major radius in m, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m is the vacuum permeability, $I_p$ is the plasma current in A, $B_p$ is the poloidal magnetic field in T, and the integral is over the plasma volume. A higher $l_i$ indicates a more centrally peaked current profile, while a lower $l_i$ corresponds to a broader current distribution. This quantity is dimensionless and is a critical parameter for vertical stability analysis and plasma control: high $l_i$ plasmas are generally more vertically stable. Typical tokamak values range from $l_i \sim 0.7$ (broad current) to $l_i \sim 1.5$ (peaked current) in standard H-mode scenarios. It is routinely extracted from equilibrium reconstruction codes using magnetic diagnostic measurements.

name: toroidal_beta
description: Ratio of volume-averaged plasma pressure to the magnetic pressure of the vacuum toroidal field at the geometric axis.
documentation: Toroidal beta is a dimensionless figure of merit for plasma confinement efficiency, defined as $\beta_t = \frac{2\mu_0 \langle p \rangle}{B_0^2}$, where $\langle p \rangle = \frac{1}{V}\int p\, dV$ is the volume-averaged total perpendicular plasma pressure (in Pa), $B_0$ is the vacuum toroidal magnetic field at the geometric axis (in T), $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m is the vacuum permeability, and $V$ is the plasma volume. Higher $\beta_t$ indicates more efficient use of the confining magnetic field. In conventional tokamak H-mode plasmas, $\beta_t$ typically ranges from 1% to 5%, while advanced tokamak and spherical tokamak discharges can reach $\beta_t \sim 10$–$40\%$. The Troyon limit provides an empirical upper bound: $\beta_t^{\max}(\%) \approx \beta_N \frac{I_p}{a B_0}$, where $\beta_N \approx 2.8$ is the normalized beta coefficient, $I_p$ is the plasma current, and $a$ is the minor radius. Toroidal beta is derived from equilibrium reconstruction constrained by kinetic profile data and magnetic measurements.
